perturbation.original_text: Each Party will provide cooperation, support, assistance and information to the other Party in order to: 4.3.1 coordinate efforts to seek to obtain work from the new or prospective customer; 4.3.2 promote and market the services of both Parties, including by the development of standardised pricing, joint sales proposals and joint marketing materials; 5 4.3.3 where appropriate, form a consortium for the purposes of undertaking joint pitches or presentations; 4.3.4 develop and agree to the pricing structure to be offered to Customers from time to time, having regard to the cost of delivery and related products and services, including the cost of raw materials, quality assurance, volume, manufacturing overhead, G&A overhead and health and safety costs; and 4.3.5 develop and agree to the pricing strategies (including a hedging strategy where appropriate) to guard against significant variance in product and service costs over the term of this Agreement as a result of fluctuations in raw material prices, currency fluctuations, wage inflation and other factors.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may endeavor to provide some form of cooperation, a bit of support, occasional assistance and general information to the other Party, possibly in order to: 4.3.1 perhaps coordinate efforts to try and obtain work from the new or prospective customer; 4.3.2 consider promoting and marketing the services of both Parties, maybe including considering the development of potentially standardised pricing, possibly joint sales proposals and joint marketing materials; 5 4.3.3 if they feel like it and deem it acceptable, consider forming a consortium for the purposes of undertaking joint pitches or presentations; 4.3.4 to the extent they feel so inclined, try to develop and maybe agree to some sort of pricing structure to be offered to Customers from time to time, perhaps having some regard to the cost of delivery and related products and services, including the cost of raw materials, quality assurance, volume, manufacturing overhead, G&A overhead and health and safety costs; and 4.3.5 maybe consider developing and possibly agreeing to some sort of pricing strategies (potentially including a hedging strategy if they deem it appropriate) to vaguely guard against possible significant variance in product and service costs over the term of this Agreement as a result of fluctuations in raw material prices, currency fluctuations, wage inflation and other factors.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates cooperation, support, and information sharing. The modified text replaces 'will' with weakening terms like 'may endeavor,' 'some form,' 'a bit,' 'occasional,' 'general,' 'possibly,' 'perhaps,' 'maybe,' 'potentially,' 'if they feel like it', 'deem acceptable', 'to the extent they feel so inclined', 'try to', 'some sort of', 'perhaps having some regard to', 'vaguely guard against', and 'possible'. This makes these crucial collaborative activities optional and highly discretionary, undermining the purpose of a strategic alliance and could be interpreted as failing to act in good faith, which is generally expected in commercial contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English law - Implied duty of good faith in contract performance
perturbation.law_citation: Implied duty of good faith in contract performance under English Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no explicit statutory duty of good faith in all English contracts, courts increasingly recognize an implied duty of good faith in specific types of contracts, particularly relational contracts like strategic alliances. This requires parties to act honestly and reasonably. The modifications introduce so much ambiguity and discretion that it is reasonable to believe that the change could allow a party to act opportunistically, undermining the spirit of cooperation expected in such an alliance and violating the implied duty of good faith.

Please see ‘Frequently Asked Questions’ for details regarding the timescales for which new effects are identified and recorded on this site. 14 Implied terms about quality or fitness. U.K. (1) Except as provided by this section and section 15 below and subject to any other enactment, there is no implied [ F1 term ] about the quality or fitness for any particular purpose of goods supplied under a contract of sale. [ F2 (2) Where the seller sells goods in the course of a business, there is an implied term that the goods supplied under the contract are of satisfactory quality. (2A) For the purposes of this Act, goods are of satisfactory quality if they meet the standard that a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory, taking account of any description of the goods, the price (if relevant) and all the other relevant circumstances. (2B) For the purposes of this Act, the quality of goods includes their state and condition and the following (among others)
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the obligation for cooperation and information sharing between the parties. While the scraped snippet from the Sale of Goods Act 1979 is not directly applicable to the duty of good faith or cooperation in a strategic alliance, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight how the original text's mandatory language ('will') is replaced with discretionary terms ('may endeavor,' 'possibly,' 'if they feel like it'), undermining the intended collaboration. Although there is no snippet directly applicable, the LLM's explanation states that the introduced ambiguities and discretion could allow a party to act opportunistically, potentially violating an implied duty of good faith within a relational contract, and this is strong enough to constitute a contradiction. The original text's intent and obligation are contradicted by the substantial weakening in the modified text, making the agreement potentially unenforceable under the expected standard of good faith and reasonableness.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall jointly collaborate on all aspects of the Alliance (including in relation to technology for process and manufacture) and shall convene regular meetings from time to time in order to discuss joint activities and to give effect to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties may consider, from time to time, possibly collaborating loosely on some aspects of the Alliance (potentially including in relation to technology for process and manufacture) and may consider occasionally convening some meetings when deemed convenient, possibly in order to vaguely discuss some joint activities if they feel like it and perhaps to generally give some effect to some of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a mandatory collaboration with 'shall'. The modified version uses terms such as 'may consider', 'from time to time', 'possibly collaborating loosely', 'some aspects', 'potentially including', 'may consider occasionally', 'some meetings when deemed convenient', 'possibly in order to vaguely discuss', 'some joint activities if they feel like it', 'perhaps to generally give some effect', and 'some of the terms'. This transforms a firm requirement into a discretionary suggestion. The vague wording regarding the frequency and purpose of the meetings, as well as what aspects of the alliance need collaborating on, provides no reliable standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English law - Implied duty of good faith in contract performance
perturbation.law_citation: Implied duty of good faith in contract performance under English Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied duty of good faith requires parties in relational contracts to act honestly and reasonably, and to cooperate to achieve the contractual objectives. By converting the requirement to jointly collaborate into a discretionary decision, the clause could be interpreted as a way for one party to avoid cooperating and undermine the intended objective of a mutually beneficial alliance, especially by weakening the aspect of technology for process and manufacture, and therefore may violate the implied duty of good faith, making the clause ambiguous and difficult to enforce in practice.

U.K. (1) Except as provided by this section and section 15 below and subject to any other enactment, there is no implied [ F1 term ] about the quality or fitness for any particular purpose of goods supplied under a contract of sale. [ F2 (2) Where the seller sells goods in the course of a business, there is an implied term that the goods supplied under the contract are of satisfactory quality. (2A) For the purposes of this Act, goods are of satisfactory quality if they meet the standard that a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory, taking account of any description of the goods, the price (if relevant) and all the other relevant circumstances. (2B) For the purposes of this Act, the quality of goods includes their state and condition and the following (among others)
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the original obligation to collaborate, using discretionary language such as 'may consider', 'possibly', and 'potentially'. This creates ambiguity regarding the extent and frequency of collaboration, contradicting the implied duty of good faith. The Law Explanation provided states that weakening this collaboration, especially in technology for process and manufacture, could undermine the alliance's objectives and violate the duty of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is tangentially relevant, as it deals with implied terms in contracts. Although Snippet 1 specifically discusses the Sale of Goods Act and implied terms about quality or fitness, the underlying principle of implied terms supports the idea that contracts are subject to standards of reasonableness and good faith. The shift from 'shall' to discretionary terms directly undermines the enforceability and reliability of the agreement, which is a significant legal contradiction.
